title: A Review of Official Unicode SignWriting
subtitle: The Character Problem and the Written-Sign Problem
summary: Official Unicode SignWriting is useful as character standardization but incomplete as a compatible written-sign production path.
synopsis: The accepted Unicode SignWriting design and later assumptions around it are valuable at the character-repertoire and script-recognition layer, but incomplete and not currently compatible at the written-sign layer for current Sutton SignWriting production use.

slides.title: Standards Distinction
slides.summary: Official Unicode SignWriting solved a real character-inventory problem but did not solve the full written-sign text model problem.
bullets: Current production use depends on other layers
slides.speech: This standards review begins with a distinction. Official Unicode SignWriting was real and valuable at the character-inventory layer, but it did not solve the full written-sign text model problem for current Sutton SignWriting production use.
slides.notes: Keep the review respectful and precise.

image_prompts.concrete: Standards review slide separating Unicode character inventory from written-sign text model and current production use layers
image_prompts.abstract: A standards layer and a text-model layer held apart for clarity

slides.title: Character Problem
slides.summary: Unicode can encode characters and script identity without automatically preserving composed written signs as usable text.
bullets: Script identity is not the whole model
Fonts do not define authored text
Rendering cannot carry the whole burden
slides.speech: The character problem is that Unicode can encode characters and script identity without automatically preserving composed written signs as usable text. Characters, fonts, rendering, and written sign structure must not be collapsed.
slides.notes: This is the key standards-layer point.

image_prompts.concrete: Technical standards slide showing Unicode characters, script identity, fonts, rendering, and written sign structure as separate boxes
image_prompts.abstract: Characters and complete written signs separated by a clear analytic boundary

slides.title: Written-Sign Problem
slides.summary: The unresolved issue is how to preserve symbol identity, sign boundaries, spatial placement, and compatibility with actual production practice.
bullets: Compatibility with FSW and SWU practice
slides.speech: The written-sign problem asks how the system preserves symbol identity, sign boundaries, spatial placement, and compatibility with actual production practice. That is a text-model problem, not only a character-allocation problem.

image_prompts.concrete: Written-sign model slide showing symbol identity, sign boundaries, spatial placement, and compatibility with FSW and SWU practice
image_prompts.abstract: A composed written sign held together by identity, boundary, and space

slides.title: Review Conclusion
slides.summary: The Unicode record should be preserved as partial standardization, not overread as a complete solution to SignWriting text.
bullets: Keep standards and production layers distinct
slides.speech: The conclusion is not to dismiss Unicode SignWriting. The conclusion is to preserve it as partial standardization while avoiding the overreading that it solved the full written-sign model.

image_prompts.concrete: Balanced standards conclusion slide preserving Unicode SignWriting as partial standardization while marking unresolved text-model gaps
image_prompts.abstract: A partial standard carefully placed inside a larger standards map
